verbatim: Chaerestratus and his supporters impugned the protestation, declaring that both Antidorus and his sister were illegitimate and that the law ordains that an illegitimate son or daughter cannot inherit as next-of-kin.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The speaker cites a specific Athenian law that prohibits children born outside of a legitimate marriage from inheriting property as next-of-kin. This is a general legal rule used to challenge the opponents' claim.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to dispose of property by will
verbatim: But, since it is lawful for any Athenian who wishes to do so to dispute an inheritance by bringing a direct action before you, and if he can establish a better claim, to obtain possession of the estate

This law, gentlemen, holds good for all men alike, permitting anyone to dispose of his property in default of male issue, providing that, at the time of doing so, he is not insane or mentally incapacitated by old age or any other of the causes mentioned in the law.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly quotes and discusses the law that allows Athenian citizens without male issue to adopt an heir and bequeath property, provided they are of sound mind.

verbatim: for no one ever makes a gift by will of anything to the sons of his own body, because the law of itself gives his father's estate to the son and does not even allow anyone who has legitimate children to dispose of his property.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage explains that under Athenian law, legitimate biological sons inherit automatically, and their existence actually restricts the father's right to dispose of the estate to anyone else by will.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to equal share of estate
verbatim: And why did he introduce them on special terms, when the law ordains that all the legitimate sons have an equal right to share in their father's property?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The speaker references a legal requirement that all legitimate sons must receive an equal portion of their father's inheritance.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to return to original family
verbatim: for the law does not allow the return of an adopted son to his original family, unless he leaves a legitimate son in the family which he quits.
extracted_rules.reasoning: This is a specific legal rule regarding adoption in Athens: an adopted person cannot reclaim their original family ties/inheritance unless they have provided a biological heir to the adopting family.

verbatim: For obviously, if Euctemon had a legitimate son, his daughter could not be heiress or the estate adjudicable.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In Athenian law, a daughter only becomes an 'epikleros' (heiress) if there are no legitimate biological or adopted sons; the presence of a son legally bars her from this status.

verbatim: Thus the contrary has been done of that which the law has prescribed; for according to the law no male or female bastard has any right, based on kinship, to participate in the cults or property of a family since the archonship of Eucleides 403-402 B.C.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author cites a specific Athenian law established after 403/2 BCE that explicitly bars children born outside of legitimate marriage (nothos/nothe) from claiming a share in the family estate or religious rites.

verbatim: The law here cited must have been that which excluded slaves and women of immoral life from participating in the festival of the Thesmophoria celebrated in honor of Demeter and Persephone... Yet the mother of these young men, being admittedly a slave, and having always lived a scandalous life, who ought never to have entered the temple and seen any of the rites performed there, had the effrontery to join in the procession.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text references a law and a specific Council decree prohibiting slaves from participating in the sacred rites of the Thesmophoria, framing her entry as a legal and religious violation.

verbatim: whether Philoctemon's sister, formerly the wife of Chaereas and now a widow, ought to pass into the power of our opponents and be married to anyone they choose or else be allowed to grow old in widowhood, or whether, as a legitimate daughter, she ought to be subject to your decision as to whom she ought to marry.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage describes the status of an epikleros (heiress). As a legitimate daughter without brothers, her marriage and the control of her estate are subject to legal adjudication by the city (the jury's decision) rather than her own choice.

verbatim: How could he be childless, when he adopted and was survived by his own nephew, to whom the law gives the right of inheritance just as much as to children of his own body?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author explicitly states that Athenian law grants an adopted son the same legal standing and inheritance rights as a biological child.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to a share of the estate
verbatim: Indeed, it is expressly stated in the law that, if children are born subsequently to one who has adopted a son, each child takes his share of the estate and both classes of children alike inherit.
extracted_rules.reasoning: This rule clarifies that an adoption is not invalidated by the later birth of biological children; instead, the law mandates that the estate be shared among all children regardless of biological or adoptive status.

verbatim: Both of them have paid all the special war-taxes, being numbered among the three hundred. i.e., the richest class. Formerly only these two members of the family contributed, but now the younger son here is choregus in the tragic competitions and has been enrolled among the three hundred and pays the war-tax.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies 'the three hundred' as the wealthiest class who are legally required to pay special war taxes and perform liturgies.
